Contact Rare & Manuscript Collections

Rose Goldsen papers, [ca. 1950-1985]
Collection Number: 14-25-1649

Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections
Cornell University Library

DESCRIPTIVE SUMMARY

Title:
Rose Goldsen papers, [ca. 1950-1985]
Repository:
Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections
Collection Number:
14-25-1649
Abstract:
Subject files containing research materials, writings, correspondence, and administrative files. There are also tape recordings, audio cassettes, and video cassettes of radio and television broadcasts, and photographs (primarily from Latin America). Subjects include public opinion, mass media, measurement of attitudes and values, economic development (Latin America), and social science methodology.
Creator:
Goldsen, Rose Kohn.
Quanitities:
79.3 cubic feet.
Language:
Collection material in English
BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE

Professor of Sociology, Cornell University.
COLLECTION DESCRIPTION

Subject files containing research materials, writings, correspondence, and administrative files. There are also tape recordings, audio cassettes, and video cassettes of radio and television broadcasts, and photographs (primarily from Latin America). Subjects include public opinion, mass media, measurement of attitudes and values, economic development (Latin America), and social science methodology.
Also, photocopy transcripts of 11 broadcasts on sociology over Ithaca, New York radio stations, February to May, 1974.
INFORMATION FOR USERS
Cite As:

Rose Goldsen papers, #14-25-1649. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.
Conditions Governing Use

Due to the fragility and potential degradation of moving image and sound recordings, viewing and listening is limited to items that have beeen digitized. If an item is in another media format, you may request to have the item digitized for access. Information on ordering access copies may be found on the Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections webpage. Audio visual material that has been digitized: TR05611a
SUBJECTS

Places:
Latin America -- Economic conditions -- 1945-
Subjects:
Women college teachers.
Sociologists.
Social sciences -- Research.
Social sciences -- Methodology.
Attitude (Psychology)
Mass media -- Research.
Public opinion.
Sociology -- Research.
Sociology -- Study and teaching.

Published Materials (1947-1984)

Access columns 6/4/79-10/20/80
Scope and Contents
["Television Commercials and Involuntary Learning" 6/4/79; "Now That You've Seer the Picture, Why Not Watch the Book?" 10/8/79; "Time Compression: Speeding Up the Spiels" 10/20/80]

Daily News-Miner (Fairbanks, Alaska) columns - with correspondence 2/79-2/80
Scope and Contents
[TV and preschool play 2/17/7S media empires 2/24; Death in the West 3/3; "SM moves to mass media" 3/24; "Pseudo-events merit too much local news" 3/31; Three-Mile Island 4/21; "Can intelligent life survive at ABC?" 5/5; "New golden age of broadcasting.." 5/19; "TV education becomes Disney-ized 5/26; TV and computers 6/9; "Author Stone's book review bureaucracy" 6/16; "ABC buys lion's share of conglomerate" 9/29; "Belly buttons, bottoms, bosoms.." 10/13; "More ways to skin bank robbers" 11/3; "Fool your: senses with TV technology" 12/1; "Advertising looks at brain research" 1/19/80; "Lt. Columbo cracks the toilet paper case" 2/2]
Box 2 Folder 3
In These Times columns - with Pat Aufderheide correspondence 7/30/79-3/12/80
Scope and Contents
["We interrupt this commercial" 7/30/79; "Public interest is in the mind of the broadcaster, says FCC" 8/22; "See the movie, watch the book" 10/17; "Into the brain with ads" 1/30/80; "Columbo can tell the difference" 3/12; "Surely God approves" mailed 1/7/80]

Ithaca New Times - correspondence (Loretta Scott) and articles 1974
Scope and Contents
["Sex Manuals Revisited" 8/25; "Selling the Big Blue Marble" 9/8; "Embryo Sweepstakes - The No Win Situation" not published]

Scope and Contents

Ithaca New Times - Jan-June, 1977
Scope and Contents
["Family Hour: Constitutional?" 1/16; "TV: The Last Violent Straw" 2/: "'Roots' Impact Defused By Ads" 2/10; "TV Violence Backfires" 2/24; "'Network1 Reflects Reality" 3/3; "Hard Sell In Toyland" 3/10; "The Shogun Lesson" 3/24; "Opening the Mikes to retired Politicos" 3/31; "Cracker Jack Wrist Slaps" 4/7; "The Pitchmen and the Public Good" 4/14; "Ministry of Taste" 4/21; "Electronic Psych" 4/28; "A Plug-In Drug" 5/5; "TV's Angels Heralding Porn" 5/12; "Sneaky Smut Seeps In" 5/19; "A Grade School Lobby" 5/26; "All Items Sold Separately" 6/2; "Presence Fashion Dolls" 6/9; "Public Access Proposal" 6/16; "Free Market of Images" 6/23; "ACT, A Parents Lobby" 6/30]
Box 2 Folder 7
Ithaca New Times - July-Dec, 1977
Scope and Contents
["Pooh-Poohed Rights" 7/7; "A Raggedy Ann Blitz" 8/2; "Rerun Diplomacy" 8/9; "Naming Presidents" 8/30; "Soap' Bubbles Dirty" 9/27; "Images of Insanity" 10/4; "Watching Winking Dots" 10/11; "Watching Zamora Trial" 10/18; "Inside the Midway" 10/25; "Hustling My Image" 11/8; "My Last Talk Show" 11/15; "Class TV - No Thanks" 11/22; "Mind-Forming Power" 11/29; "Flight Out of Egypt" 12/6; "A Tip from Sweden" 12/13; "Thirty-Second Sex" 12/20; "Once Upon A Time" 12/27]
Box 2 Folder 8
Ithaca New Times - Jan-May, 1978
Scope and Contents
["Technically, Hype" 2/7?; "'Truth in Advertising" 2/7?; "Learning to Watch" 2/14; "Spaced Out Brain Waves" 2/21; "TV: The Brain Shredder" 2/28; "The Laws of Looking" 3/7; "FTC Drops a Bombshell" 3/14; "Celebrity Sweepstakes" 3/28; "Broadcasters Play God" 4/4; "Living Room Gambling" 4/18; "Holocaust: What Next?" 4/25 - incl. fan mail; "The Media Message" 5/9; "Non-Taxable Spectacle" 5/16; "The Born Innocent Case" 5/23]

Colombo Cracks the Toilet Paper Case" 10/12; "The Osmond Empire: From Cattle to Clothes" 10/19; "Computer Controversy" 11/2; "Bill Cosby: Brand Loyalty" 11/9; "Surely God Approves" 11/23 "Northern Lights: TV Beams in Alaska" 12/7; "No Television: Cold Turkey Treatment" 12/14; "Jonestown Massacre: 'Event' of the Year" 12/21]

Ithaca Times - Jan-Sept, 1979 [TV in the Nursery: The Take-over of the Imagination" 1/18; "Censorship in Marlboro Country" 1/25; "Mattel Takes Over Western" 2/1 "Corporate Press, Global Reach" 2/8; "Corporate School Text Packs: A Ton of Garbage" 2/15; "He-e-e-ere's Congress! Hollywood in the House" 3/1; S&M Chic Socks I to Women in Vogue" 3/22; "TV: Rhythmic Rhetoric Managin the News" 3/29; "Nuclear Crisis, Stock Market Bonanza" 4/5; "Expert Reveals: Just a Reactor Excursion" 4/12; "Sneak Preview: ABC's Prime-Time Lineup" 4/26; "Disneyland Goes to College" 5/10; "Mickey Mouse Regresses: Evolution in Reverse" 5/17; "TV & Computers: Can We Kick the Habits?" 5/31; "Corporate Bureaucracies The Principle of the Passed Buck" 6/28" "Do Broadcaster Violate Anti-Trust Laws?" 8/30; "The Public Interest: Once Again Up for Grabs" 9/13; "Now that You've Seen the Picture, Why Not Watch the Book?" 9/27]

Book reviews - with correspondence 1974-75
Scope and Contents

Schon), Administrative Science Quarterly 9/75]

Book reviews - with correspondence 1978-80

Conceptual Subject Files
Evolution And Habitat
Box 34 Folder 1
Some notes on evolution of physical organisms
Box 34 Folder 2
Human evolution, animal evolution
Box 34 Folder 3
Physical structures are not our only habitat - although they are revealing.
Box 34 Folder 4
Each generation is evolved by & evolves its own habitat
Box 34 Folder 5
Evolution of social loops: examples and RKG notes
Box 34 Folder 6
Umwelt - barn swallows, David in plastic bubble, embryo implants, supermarkets, breastfeeding
Box 34 Folder 7
Bureaucracy creates a habitat.
Social System, Culture And History
Box 34 Folder 8
Property of a system versus characteristics of an individual (a necessary preface to understanding the concept of culture)
Box 34 Folder 9
System and process - some examples of confusion betweewn micromotives and system
Box 34 Folder 10
Homo culturus - social circuitry
Box 34 Folder 11
Culture/system as process (circuitries) - MacLuhan, Watts, Bateson
Box 34 Folder 12
Sex is circuitry. Making love (human sex) is reciprocal noticing as well as mating.
Scope and Contents
[File includes Legitimizing homosexuality. ]
Box 34 Folder 13
Only if you understand that the social system is process, not mass, can you understand what it means t say Copernicus changed man's world; and those photographs showing our planet hurtling through space...?
Box 34 Folder 14
What do you look at if you want to "see" the social system? Where do you look? Status, role, norms, rules- all are reciprocal; all are circuitries.
Box 34 Folder 15
Expectations lie in social system: power-authority; status-role; norms-legitimacy; options-power.
Box 34 Folder 16
Concept of culture - Cultures can die as their relational systems are killed.
Box 34 Folder 17
Cultures can die as their relational systems are knocked out. (Their loops are torn.)
Box 34 Folder 18
Cultures can die as their relational systems are killed - examples.
Box 34 Folder 19
History de-codifies.
Box 34 Folder 20
History and time and cultural integrity
Box 34 Folder 21
History, relativism, compromise
Box 34 Folder 22
History is destroyed

Series IX. Audio Tapes
Scope and Contents
RKG Radio Programs Unless otherwise indicated, tape speed is 1/2 inches per second (ips). Different programs may have the title. An asterisk * after a title means that the script for the broadcast is on file in Box 4 of the collection. Differences in dates or substantial differences in title between the tape and he script are indicated in brackets.
